title: Japanese Zaru Soba (Cold Buckwheat Noodles)

ingredients.name: soba noodles (buckwheat noodles)
visual_checks: use 100% buckwheat for gluten-free; most commercial soba contains some wheat
ingredients.notes: use 100% buckwheat for gluten-free; most commercial soba contains some wheat

subs.name: 100% buckwheat soba for a gluten-free version; check the label as many brands blend with wheat flour
subs.notes: in India, look for 'sobha noodles' at Japanese or Korean import stores

ingredients.name: kombu (dried kelp), for dipping sauce
visual_checks: a 10 cm strip; makes the cold dashi base for the dip
ingredients.notes: a 10 cm strip; makes the cold dashi base for the dip

subs.name: 1 tsp instant dashi powder dissolved in 300 ml water if kombu is unavailable — gives a very similar base for the dipping sauce
tamari (wheat-free soy sauce) for a gluten-free dipping sauce, keeping the same quantities

ingredients.name: sugar (or mirin)
visual_checks: balances the salt of the soy
ingredients.notes: balances the salt of the soy

subs.name: crushed roasted peanuts for a different but pleasant flavour note alongside the dipping sauce

ingredients.name: toasted sesame oil
visual_checks: a few drops tossed through the drained noodles prevent sticking and add fragrance
ingredients.notes: a few drops tossed through the drained noodles prevent sticking and add fragrance

ingredients.name: water
visual_checks: for the dipping sauce dashi and for boiling noodles
ingredients.notes: for the dipping sauce dashi and for boiling noodles

steps.text: Make the dipping sauce: combine 300 ml water and the kombu strip in a small saucepan. Bring slowly to a simmer over low heat — 10 minutes. Remove the kombu, add soy sauce and sugar, stir and simmer for 1 more minute. Pour into a bowl and refrigerate until completely cold, at least 20 minutes.

Bring a large pot of water to a boil (no salt — soba noodles need unsalted water). Add the soba noodles and cook for 3 to 5 minutes per the package timing, stirring occasionally to prevent sticking. Taste one at 3 minutes — it should be tender with just the faintest resistance.

Drain the noodles immediately and plunge them into a large bowl of ice water. Rub the noodles gently with your hands under the cold water — this removes the surface starch and is essential for the characteristic clean, non-sticky texture.
Drain the chilled noodles very well in a colander, shaking out as much water as possible. If using sesame oil, drizzle it over and toss lightly to coat each strand.

Arrange the cold noodles in a mound on a plate or in a bowl. Pour the chilled dipping sauce into small cups or a shallow bowl on the side. Serve with spring onion and sesame seeds in separate small dishes — diners add them to the dip according to taste.
To eat: pick up a small bundle of noodles, dip the ends briefly into the sauce (not the whole strand), and eat in one or two slurps. The noodles should stay cold throughout the meal — work quickly.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: noodles → 100% buckwheat soba for a gluten-free version; check the label as many brands blend with wheat flour (in India, look for 'sobha noodles' at Japanese or Korean import stores); seaweed → 1 tsp instant dashi powder dissolved in 300 ml water if kombu is unavailable — gives a very similar base for the dipping sauce; soy-sauce → tamari (wheat-free soy sauce) for a gluten-free dipping sauce, keeping the same quantities; sesame-seeds → crushed roasted peanuts for a different but pleasant flavour note alongside the dipping sauce
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Soba noodles are available at Korean and Japanese import stores in Indian metros and online. The recipe requires only a saucepan and no special technique beyond chilling. Instant dashi powder (sold alongside soba noodles at import stores) makes the dipping sauce fast. If kombu is unavailable, a light soy-based sauce with a pinch of seaweed flakes also works. Serve on a hot summer day as an alternative to any Indian cold meal.
